---
title: "Results"
---

## Overview

The results address the central question: **do firms that the government selects for less-competitive mechanisms have lower costs than firms that only compete in public auctions?** The answer depends on the type of project. For complex contracts involving sewage work, the answer is yes, the selected firms are genuinely more efficient. For simple pavement contracts (the majority), the answer is no, selected firms are actually more expensive.

This section presents the results in three parts: (1) the cost CDF comparison from the structural model, (2) the efficiency index estimates from SFA, and (3) the geographic distribution of efficiency across Mexican states.

---

## Part 1: Cost CDF Comparison

### Reading a CDF in This Context

The cumulative distribution function $\hat{F}_j(c)$ gives the **fraction of firms of type $j$ with pseudo-cost below $c$**. A CDF that lies to the **left** means that a larger share of firms in that group can complete the project at any given cost threshold, i.e., that type has systematically lower costs.

More formally, if $\hat{F}_1(c) > \hat{F}_0(c)$ for all $c$ in the support, then Type 1 first-order stochastically dominates Type 0 in terms of cost, meaning Type 1 draws lower costs at every quantile. When the CDFs cross, the comparison is ambiguous and depends on the part of the distribution under consideration.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## First-order stochastic dominance and what it implies for welfare

**First-order stochastic dominance (FOSD)** of Type 1 over Type 0 in cost means:
$$F_1(c) \geq F_0(c) \quad \text{for all } c$$

This implies that a randomly drawn Type 1 firm has a lower cost than a randomly drawn Type 0 firm with probability greater than 1/2. In the procurement context, it means the government's selection of Type 1 firms is, on average, cost-justified.

**Why CDFs at multiple quantiles?** The paper reports CDFs at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of the project-size distribution. This controls for project size, since larger contracts may have systematically different cost structures. The toggle below shows CDFs at the median project size (most representative).

**Why CDF comparisons rather than mean costs?** Means can be sensitive to outliers, especially when pseudo-costs may have kernel estimation error in the tails. CDFs are more robust and allow visual inspection of the entire distribution.
:::

### Cost CDFs: Simple vs. Complex Projects

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

# --- Simple projects: data_densities.csv, drenaje == 0 ---
df_simple = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "data_densities.csv")
df_simple = df_simple[df_simple["drenaje"] == 0].dropna(subset=["c_hat", "F50", "typeb"])
df_simple = df_simple.sort_values("c_hat")

simple_t0 = df_simple[df_simple["typeb"] == 0].sort_values("c_hat")
simple_t1 = df_simple[df_simple["typeb"] == 1].sort_values("c_hat")

# --- Complex projects: data_densities_drenaje.csv, drenaje == 1 ---
df_complex = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "data_densities_drenaje.csv")
df_complex = df_complex[df_complex["drenaje"] == 1].dropna(subset=["c_hat", "F50_d1", "typeb"])
df_complex = df_complex.sort_values("c_hat")

complex_t0 = df_complex[df_complex["typeb"] == 0].sort_values("c_hat")
complex_t1 = df_complex[df_complex["typeb"] == 1].sort_values("c_hat")

colors = {"t0": "#2E75B6", "t1": "#F2A900"}

fig = go.Figure()

# Persistent dummy traces so legend stays visible across toggles (indices 0–1)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=[None], y=[None], mode="lines",
    name="Type 0 — Public-auction-only",
    line=dict(color=colors["t0"], width=2.5),
    showlegend=True, visible=True
))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=[None], y=[None], mode="lines",
    name="Type 1 — Government-selected",
    line=dict(color=colors["t1"], width=2.5, dash="dash"),
    showlegend=True, visible=True
))

# Simple traces (visible by default, indices 2–3)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=simple_t0["c_hat"], y=simple_t0["F50"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 0 — Public-auction-only",
    line=dict(color=colors["t0"], width=2.5),
    visible=True, showlegend=False
))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=simple_t1["c_hat"], y=simple_t1["F50"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 1 — Government-selected",
    line=dict(color=colors["t1"], width=2.5, dash="dash"),
    visible=True, showlegend=False
))

# Complex traces (hidden by default, indices 4–5)
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=complex_t0["c_hat"], y=complex_t0["F50_d1"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 0 — Public-auction-only",
    line=dict(color=colors["t0"], width=2.5),
    visible=False, showlegend=False
))
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=complex_t1["c_hat"], y=complex_t1["F50_d1"],
    mode="lines", name="Type 1 — Government-selected",
    line=dict(color=colors["t1"], width=2.5, dash="dash"),
    visible=False, showlegend=False
))

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text="Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Simple Pavement Projects (no sewage)",
        font=dict(size=14, color="#012169"),
        y=0.97, x=0.0, xanchor="left"
    ),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="Pseudo-cost (thousands MXN per m³, 2018 prices)",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee"
    ),
    yaxis=dict(
        title="Cumulative probability F(c)",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee",
        range=[0, 1]
    ),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0, y=-0.22, font=dict(size=12)),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=440,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=80, b=110),
    updatemenus=[dict(
        type="dropdown",
        x=0.0, y=1.0,
        xanchor="left", yanchor="bottom",
        showactive=True,
        buttons=[
            dict(
                label="Simple (no sewage)",
                method="update",
                args=[
                    {"visible": [True, True, True, True, False, False]},
                    {"title.text": "Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Simple Pavement Projects (no sewage)"}
                ]
            ),
            dict(
                label="Complex (with sewage)",
                method="update",
                args=[
                    {"visible": [True, True, False, False, True, True]},
                    {"title.text": "Cost CDFs by Firm Type — Complex Pavement Projects (with sewage)"}
                ]
            )
        ]
    )]
)

fig.show()
```

### Interpreting the CDF Results

**Simple projects (no sewage, ~62% of public contracts):** The Type 0 CDF lies to the **left** of the Type 1 CDF. This means that at every cost threshold, a larger share of Type 0 firms can complete the project at or below that cost. Government-selected firms (Type 1) are less cost-efficient for routine pavement work. The government would obtain lower-cost contractors by defaulting to public auctions for these contracts.

**Complex projects (with sewage, ~38% of public contracts):** The pattern **reverses**. The Type 1 CDF lies to the left of the Type 0 CDF. Government-selected firms have lower costs when the project involves sewage infrastructure. This is consistent with **economies of scope**: firms with prior experience in multi-task contracts (combining pavement with drainage or utility work) have lower costs for complex projects, even after conditioning on project characteristics.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why does the result flip for complex projects?

One interpretation is that the government's selection criterion is (partially) valid for complex projects but not for simple ones. A second interpretation is that Type 1 firms have invested in capabilities, including equipment, workforce, and project management, that are only cost-advantageous when projects involve multiple types of work. For homogeneous pavement without sewage, their specialized advantages disappear, and their higher overhead makes them more expensive.

A third possibility is reverse causality: governments learned, over time, which firms perform well on complex projects and selectively directed those firms toward I3P contracts. If so, the efficiency advantage of Type 1 firms on complex projects is partly a consequence of the selection process itself, as governments learned by doing. The structural model cannot distinguish between these interpretations, but all of them are consistent with the finding.
:::

---

## Part 2: Stochastic Frontier Efficiency Index

The SFA estimates a **cost frontier**, the minimum cost achievable given output and input prices, and recovers firm-level deviations from it. The Battese-Coelli index $BC = e^{-\hat{\eta}} \in (0,1]$ summarizes efficiency, with higher values indicating less excess cost.

### Efficiency Index Distributions by Firm Type

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde
import plotly.graph_objects as go

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

df_eff = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "efficiency_index_comparison_drenaje_sfa.csv")
df_eff = df_eff.dropna(subset=["bc_h", "typeb"])

n_t0 = (df_eff["typeb"] == 0).sum()
n_t1 = (df_eff["typeb"] == 1).sum()

colors = {0: "#2E75B6", 1: "#F2A900"}
labels = {
    0: f"Type 0 — Public-auction-only (n={n_t0})",
    1: f"Type 1 — Government-selected (n={n_t1})"
}
line_styles = {0: "solid", 1: "dash"}

fig = go.Figure()

for t in [0, 1]:
    vals = df_eff[df_eff["typeb"] == t]["bc_h"].values
    kde = gaussian_kde(vals, bw_method="scott")
    x = np.linspace(0.08, 1.0, 500)
    y = kde(x)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=x, y=y,
        mode="lines",
        name=labels[t],
        line=dict(color=colors[t], width=2.5, dash=line_styles[t]),
        fill="tozeroy",
        fillcolor=f"rgba({int(colors[t][1:3],16)},{int(colors[t][3:5],16)},{int(colors[t][5:7],16)},0.07)"
    ))

# Add vertical lines at means
for t in [0, 1]:
    mean_val = df_eff[df_eff["typeb"] == t]["bc_h"].mean()
    fig.add_vline(
        x=mean_val,
        line=dict(color=colors[t], width=1.5, dash="dot"),
        annotation_text=f"Mean: {mean_val:.3f}",
        annotation_position="top right" if t == 1 else "top left",
        annotation_font=dict(size=10, color=colors[t])
    )

fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(
        text="BC Efficiency Index Distribution by Firm Type (Half-Normal SFA, Complex Projects)",
        font=dict(size=14, color="#012169")
    ),
    xaxis=dict(
        title="BC Efficiency Index = exp(-η̂)   [range: 0 to 1;  higher = more efficient]",
        tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee",
        range=[0.08, 1.0]
    ),
    yaxis=dict(title="Density", tickfont=dict(size=12), gridcolor="#eee"),
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0, y=-0.24, font=dict(size=12)),
    plot_bgcolor="white", paper_bgcolor="white",
    height=420,
    margin=dict(l=60, r=20, t=60, b=100)
)

fig.show()
```

### Interpreting the Efficiency Index

The density plot shows the full distribution of $BC$ efficiency scores for each firm type. The vertical dotted lines mark the means.

::: {.callout-note}
## What does BC = 0.65 mean?

If a firm has a BC efficiency index of 0.65, it means that firm uses $1/0.65 = 1.54$ times the inputs that a frontier firm would use to produce the same output. In other words, it incurs **54% excess costs** relative to the best-practice firm in the sample.

This does not mean the firm is wasting resources in an obvious sense, as the frontier is estimated from the sample and reflects what is achievable given the technology and market conditions in Jalisco. A BC of 0.65 simply means there are firms in the same market producing the same output at meaningfully lower cost.
:::

The SFA results confirm the CDF patterns:

- For **complex projects**, the Type 1 distribution is shifted toward higher BC values (closer to 1), indicating less excess cost, consistent with genuine efficiency advantages
- The difference in means is statistically significant at conventional levels under both the half-normal and truncated-normal specifications

### Half-Normal vs. Truncated-Normal: Robustness

| Model | Type 0 Mean BC | Type 1 Mean BC | Difference | p-value |
|-------|---------------|---------------|------------|---------|
| Half-normal | — | — | Type 1 > Type 0 | < 0.05 |
| Truncated-normal | — | — | Type 1 > Type 0 | < 0.05 |

Both distributional assumptions yield the same qualitative conclusion. The truncated-normal model, which freely estimates the mean of the inefficiency distribution, confirms that the Type 1 efficiency advantage for complex projects is not an artifact of the half-normal's restriction.

---

## Part 3: Geographic Distribution of Efficiency

The map below shows the **average BC efficiency index by Mexican state** (weighted by contract volume). States with more contracts contribute more to their state average. Brighter colors (toward gold) indicate higher average efficiency; darker colors (navy) indicate more excess cost.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import requests

DATA_PATH = "../../code_jmp/Exploratory R/"

df_raw = pd.read_csv(DATA_PATH + "efficiency_index_sfa_drenaje.csv")
df_raw = df_raw.dropna(subset=["id_ent", "bc_h"])

# Aggregate to state level
df_map = df_raw.groupby("id_ent").agg(
    bc_mean=("bc_h", "mean"),
    n=("bc_h", "count")
).reset_index()
df_map["id_ent"] = df_map["id_ent"].astype(int)

# INEGI state code → ISO 3166-2 code mapping
inegi_to_iso = {
    1: "MX-AGU",  2: "MX-BCN",  3: "MX-BCS",  4: "MX-CAM",
    5: "MX-COA",  6: "MX-COL",  7: "MX-CHP",  8: "MX-CHH",
    9: "MX-CMX", 10: "MX-DUR", 11: "MX-GUA", 12: "MX-GRO",
   13: "MX-HID", 14: "MX-JAL", 15: "MX-MEX", 16: "MX-MIC",
   17: "MX-MOR", 18: "MX-NAY", 19: "MX-NLE", 20: "MX-OAX",
   21: "MX-PUE", 22: "MX-QUE", 23: "MX-ROO", 24: "MX-SLP",
   25: "MX-SIN", 26: "MX-SON", 27: "MX-TAB", 28: "MX-TAM",
   29: "MX-TLA", 30: "MX-VER", 31: "MX-YUC", 32: "MX-ZAC"
}

df_map["iso_code"] = df_map["id_ent"].map(inegi_to_iso)
df_map = df_map.dropna(subset=["iso_code"])

GEOJSON_URL = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/angelnmara/geojson/master/mexicoHigh.json"

try:
    r = requests.get(GEOJSON_URL, timeout=12)
    r.raise_for_status()
    geojson_data = r.json()

    # The GeoJSON uses feature-level "id" field with ISO codes like "MX-JAL"
    fig = go.Figure(go.Choropleth(
        geojson=geojson_data,
        locations=df_map["iso_code"],
        z=df_map["bc_mean"],
        colorscale=[
            [0.0, "#012169"],
            [0.4, "#2E75B6"],
            [0.7, "#8AADDA"],
            [1.0, "#F2A900"]
        ],
        zmin=df_map["bc_mean"].min(),
        zmax=df_map["bc_mean"].max(),
        marker_line_color="white",
        marker_line_width=0.8,
        colorbar=dict(
            title=dict(text="BC Index<br>(higher = more efficient)", font=dict(size=11)),
            tickfont=dict(size=10),
            len=0.65,
            thickness=15
        ),
        customdata=df_map[["iso_code", "n"]].values,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{location}</b><br>BC Index: %{z:.3f}<br>Contracts: %{customdata[1]}<extra></extra>"
    ))

    fig.update_geos(
        fitbounds="locations",
        visible=False,
        bgcolor="white"
    )

    fig.update_layout(
        title=dict(
            text="Average BC Efficiency Index by Mexican State (Half-Normal SFA)",
            font=dict(size=14, color="#012169")
        ),
        geo=dict(showframe=False, showcoastlines=False, projection_type="mercator"),
        height=500,
        margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=55, b=10),
        paper_bgcolor="white"
    )

    fig.show()

except Exception as e:
    # Fallback: bar chart by state
    df_sorted = df_map.sort_values("bc_mean", ascending=True)
    iso_labels = df_sorted["iso_code"].str.replace("MX-", "")
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
        x=df_sorted["bc_mean"],
        y=iso_labels,
        orientation="h",
        marker_color=df_sorted["bc_mean"],
        marker_colorscale=[[0.0, "#012169"], [1.0, "#F2A900"]],
        text=df_sorted["bc_mean"].round(3),
        textposition="outside"
    ))
    fig.update_layout(
        title=dict(text="Average BC Efficiency Index by State (map unavailable — bar chart shown)",
                   font=dict(size=13, color="#012169")),
        xaxis=dict(title="BC Efficiency Index", range=[0, 1]),
        yaxis=dict(title="State (ISO code)"),
        height=600,
        margin=dict(l=80, r=60, t=60, b=40),
        paper_bgcolor="white",
        plot_bgcolor="white"
    )
    fig.show()
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Interpreting the geographic variation (state map)

The state-level average reflects the composition of firms participating in public auctions in that state's municipalities. States with few contracts have less precise estimates; the contract count is available in the hover tooltip.

Large variation across states may reflect:
1. **Local market conditions:** thin contractor markets in less populated states may have fewer efficient firms
2. **Sample composition:** states with more Type 1 bidders may show higher average efficiency if Type 1 firms are more efficient in complex projects
3. **Contract mix:** states with more complex (sewage-inclusive) contracts may show different average efficiency than states with mostly simple pavement work
:::

---

## Summary of Main Results

| | Simple contracts | Complex contracts (sewage) |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 pseudo-costs vs. Type 0** | Higher (worse ✗) | Lower (better ✓) |
| **Type 1 efficiency index vs. Type 0** | Lower (worse ✗) | Higher (better ✓) |
| **CDF dominance** | Type 0 dominates | Type 1 dominates |
| **Share of I3P auctions** | ~76% | ~24% |
| **Policy implication** | Switch to public auction | Discretion may be justified |

The combined evidence from the structural model (CDFs) and the SFA (efficiency index) points consistently in the same direction. For most I3P contracts, the simple, routine pavement projects, the government would obtain lower-cost contractors by opening the process to public competition. For the minority of contracts involving sewage work, the selected firms appear to have genuine cost advantages, possibly reflecting cumulative experience with multi-task construction.
